data.title: Boeing (BA) Q1 Loss And One Off Driven EPS Challenge Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Boeing (BA) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of US$22.2b and a basic EPS loss of US$0.11, as net income excluding extra items came in at a loss of US$90m. Over the past six reported quarters, revenue has moved from US$15.2b in Q4 2024 to a range between US$19.5b and US$24.0b through 2025. Quarterly EPS has swung between a loss of US$7.14 and a profit of US$10.59, which places greater focus on margins and reported profitability than on the headline revenue line.

See our full analysis for Boeing.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the most common narratives around Boeing, and where the data pushes back against them.

See what the community is saying about Boeing

Advertisement

Trailing 12‑month profit helped by US$9.6b one off

  • Over the latest twelve months Boeing reported net income of US$1.9b and basic EPS of US$2.50, but this outcome includes a US$9.6b one off gain that materially lifts reported profitability compared with earlier periods where trailing EPS was deeply negative.
  • What bulls highlight as a clean return to profit is more mixed in the numbers, because:
    • Trailing basic EPS moved from a loss of US$18.36 per share at Q4 2024 to a profit of US$2.50 per share at Q1 2026. That swing is heavily influenced by the one off item rather than only by operating improvements.
    • Revenue over the same trailing period grew from US$66.5b at Q4 2024 to US$92.2b at Q1 2026, which supports the bullish view of a bigger business. However, the size of the non recurring gain means current margins may not reflect a steady state.

Bulls argue the latest profit run rate points to a stronger multi year recovery story, but these figures show how much of that picture is tied to a single US$9.6b gain rather than repeatable earnings, which is exactly what the bullish case needs to clear for long term comfort. 🐂 Boeing Bull Case

High 96x P/E versus US$267.84 price target

  • Boeing currently trades on a trailing P/E of 96x, compared with an industry average of 37.7x and a peer average of 27.6x, while the share price of US$234.15 sits below the allowed analyst price target of US$267.84.
  • Consensus narrative sees this as justified by strong growth expectations, yet the data gives a mixed picture:
    • Analysts expect earnings to grow about 36.9% per year and revenue about 9.4% per year, so the current 96x multiple is being supported by forecasts that earnings will rise much faster than the top line.
    • At the same time, the DCF fair value of US$322.83 is above both the current price and the US$267.84 analyst target, which supports the optimistic view on future cash flows even as the elevated P/E looks demanding versus industry norms.

Debt coverage and one offs keep bears focused on risk

  • Risk analysis flags that operating cash flow does not currently cover debt well, while the same US$9.6b one off that lifts EPS also makes recent earnings less representative of ongoing performance.
  • Critics point to these figures as support for the cautious narrative:
    • Debt being poorly covered by operating cash flow means more of future earnings may need to go to servicing obligations, which aligns with bearish concerns about financial flexibility.
    • Significant insider selling over the past three months, alongside earnings that are materially affected by a single gain, fits the skeptical view that recent profitability and valuation multiples may not fully reflect underlying business strength.

Skeptics warn that stretched debt coverage and heavy reliance on a one time gain leave less room for error if revenue or margins soften from here. 🐻 Boeing Bear Case

Next Steps

data.title: HSBC Weighs Singapore Insurance Sale And Capital Reallocation In Asia
data.description:
• HSBC Holdings (LSE:HSBA) is moving ahead with a review of its Singapore insurance arm, HSBC Life (Singapore).
  • Allianz, Daiichi Life, and Sumitomo Life have been shortlisted as bidders for the business.
  • The review forms part of a wider effort to streamline HSBC's organisation and refine its focus in Asia.

HSBC is a global bank with a large presence in Asian wealth and insurance, and Singapore is a key hub for that franchise. A potential sale of HSBC Life (Singapore) would sit alongside wider reshaping of the group, as management works on capital use and business mix across regions. For investors, the short list of major insurance groups signals clear interest in HSBC's insurance assets in the region.

Any outcome from this review could influence how HSBC positions its wealth and insurance offering in Asia and how capital is allocated within the group over time. Readers tracking LSE:HSBA may want to watch for details on transaction structure, use of any proceeds, and how CEO Georges Elhedery frames the role of insurance in HSBC's broader strategy.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for HSBC Holdings by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on HSBC Holdings.

2 things going right for HSBC Holdings that this headline doesn't cover.

The review of HSBC Life (Singapore) sits right at the intersection of HSBC’s Asia-focused wealth strategy and its ongoing reshaping of the group. A potential sale in the region of up to $2b would give HSBC more flexibility over where capital is tied up, while still keeping Singapore as a core hub for wealth and wholesale banking. With Allianz, Daiichi Life and Sumitomo Life on the shortlist, investors can see that well capitalised peers are willing to commit resources to the franchise, which may help validate the business HSBC has built. At the same time, handing over an on the ground insurance platform could affect how tightly integrated HSBC’s wealth, bancassurance and protection products are in Singapore versus competitors such as Standard Chartered or Citigroup that also lean on regional hubs.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The HSBC Holdings Narrative

  • The potential monetisation of the Singapore insurance unit is consistent with the narrative that HSBC is recycling capital from lower return or non core assets into higher return Asian wealth, trade and fee income opportunities.
  • If the unit is sold, HSBC would need to show that any lost insurance earnings can be offset by growth elsewhere. Otherwise, the story of steadily improving returns from Asia could face more questions.
  • The competitive interest from global and Japanese insurers, and the use of Singlife as a platform by Sumitomo Life, add detail on regional partnership options. This is not fully reflected in the existing narrative’s focus on internal restructuring and cost discipline.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for HSBC Holdings to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Selling a Singapore insurance business that is embedded in HSBC’s regional wealth offering could reduce cross selling potential and product depth versus banks that keep integrated insurance arms.
  • ⚠️ Execution risk around any sale process, including regulatory approvals and the terms of future distribution agreements, may affect how steady earnings from Singapore insurance look after the review is completed.
  • 🎁 A successful sale at an attractive valuation could free up capital that HSBC can direct into higher return areas in Asia and the Middle East, in line with its focus on improving group returns.
  • 🎁 Partnering with a specialist insurer such as Allianz, Daiichi Life or Sumitomo Life might allow HSBC to maintain customer access to insurance products while reducing balance sheet intensity.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, investors can watch for clarity on whether HSBC sells all or part of HSBC Life (Singapore), the headline valuation, and any long term distribution or partnership agreements that sit alongside a transaction. It is also worth tracking how management explains the impact on Asia wealth and insurance earnings, and whether any capital released is directed toward buybacks, growth investments or balance sheet strength.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a single present value figure.
